skill.name: when-auditing-security-use-security-analyzer
skill.description: Runs multi-phase security reviews with static checks, dependency audit guidance, secrets detection, OWASP checks, and Claude Flow coordination.
skill.summary: Coordinates security audits across code, dependencies, secrets, runtime checks, and OWASP coverage.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many severe alerts, but most critical patterns are security examples, scanner signatures, or workflow labels rather than malicious behavior. The active shell helper still runs external local tooling, scans broad filesystem paths, and writes possible secrets to temporary files and reports, so publication should include a clear operational warning.

medium_findings.title: Local Shell Tooling Executes During Scans
medium_findings.description: The slash command runs local shell commands and external tooling including npx claude-flow@alpha, grep, npm audit, jq, curl, and bc. This is expected for a security scanner, but it increases execution and supply-chain risk, especially because claude-flow@alpha is not pinned to an immutable version.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell script directly invokes external commands during normal operation. The commands appear related to scanning, but the unpinned npx package and broad command use require user awareness.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Findings Written To Temporary Files And Reports
medium_findings.description: The script scans for secrets and writes matching lines to /tmp files, then copies exposed secret findings into the generated report. This can expose sensitive source snippets to local users, logs, artifacts, or broad file permissions if run in shared environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script explicitly writes secret scan results to predictable temp files and includes them in the report. This is legitimate scanner behavior, but it can leak sensitive findings if artifacts are mishandled.

medium_findings.title: Broad Filesystem Read Scope For User Selected Paths
medium_findings.description: The scanner accepts a path and recursively reads matching project files for static analysis, dependency checks, and secrets detection. The path is quoted and validated as a directory, so command injection evidence was not found, but users should only run it on intended repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The recursive scans are clearly present and user path handling is mostly quoted. Risk is operational exposure of scanned content rather than confirmed malicious access.

low_findings.title: Static Alerts From Security Examples
low_findings.description: Many critical-looking alerts are vulnerable examples or detection signatures inside security documentation. Examples include eval, innerHTML, path traversal, weak crypto, API key, and password-file payloads used to teach or detect issues, not to exploit this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged snippets are presented as vulnerable examples, scanner patterns, or generated report paths. They are not executed as exploit logic by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Limited Network And External Service Touchpoints
low_findings.description: The active dynamic test checks localhost:3000, while metadata and examples include GitHub and API URLs. No evidence was found that the skill exfiltrates code or secrets to a remote endpoint, but npx and npm audit may contact package infrastructure when used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only explicit active HTTP target is localhost, and URL examples are documentation. Package tooling may use the network, so users should review network policy before running.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Review found no instruction that tells the evaluator to skip security analysis, change risk level, ignore prior instructions, or treat the skill as pre-approved. The subagent prompt describes the intended security-analyzer role and does not target the marketplace evaluator.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search found only normal configuration language and a role prompt for the documented subagent. No evaluator-directed override language was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Shell Tooling Executes During Scans
dangerous_patterns.description: The slash command runs local shell commands and external tooling including npx claude-flow@alpha, grep, npm audit, jq, curl, and bc. This is expected for a security scanner, but it increases execution and supply-chain risk, especially because claude-flow@alpha is not pinned to an immutable version.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell script directly invokes external commands during normal operation. The commands appear related to scanning, but the unpinned npx package and broad command use require user awareness.

dangerous_patterns.description: The script scans for secrets and writes matching lines to /tmp files, then copies exposed secret findings into the generated report. This can expose sensitive source snippets to local users, logs, artifacts, or broad file permissions if run in shared environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script explicitly writes secret scan results to predictable temp files and includes them in the report. This is legitimate scanner behavior, but it can leak sensitive findings if artifacts are mishandled.

dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner accepts a path and recursively reads matching project files for static analysis, dependency checks, and secrets detection. The path is quoted and validated as a directory, so command injection evidence was not found, but users should only run it on intended repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The recursive scans are clearly present and user path handling is mostly quoted. Risk is operational exposure of scanned content rather than confirmed malicious access.

content.user_title: Audit Security Risks with Coordinated Analysis
content.value_statement: Security reviews often miss issues across code, dependencies, secrets, and runtime behavior. This skill coordinates Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows to produce structured security findings and remediation guidance.
actual_capabilities: Guides static checks for SQL injection, XSS, path traversal, and weak cryptography.
Runs a shell helper that scans selected project paths with grep-based rules.
Checks npm dependency audit output when package.json is present.
Searches source files for common API key and hardcoded credential patterns.
Produces a markdown security report with severity counts and sample findings.
Coordinates Claude Flow hooks and memory entries for multi-phase audit workflows.
limitations: Dynamic testing is limited and only checks a localhost health endpoint by default.
The grep-based rules can create false positives and miss framework-specific issues.
Secret findings may include sensitive snippets in temporary files and reports.
The helper depends on local tools such as jq, npm, curl, bc, and npx.

use_cases.title: Pre-commit repository scan
use_cases.description: Run a quick local scan before merging changes to catch obvious code and dependency risks.

use_cases.title: Security review preparation
use_cases.description: Collect a first-pass report covering static issues, secrets, dependencies, and OWASP-oriented checks.

use_cases.title: CI audit artifact generation
use_cases.description: Generate a markdown report that can be attached to build logs or review tickets.

prompt_templates.title: Run a basic security review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner scan for a local project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review the current repository for common security issues. Summarize critical, high, and medium findings with remediation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Focus on secrets and dependencies
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to check for exposed secrets and vulnerable dependencies. Explain which findings need immediate action before release.

prompt_templates.title: Compare risk against a baseline
prompt_templates.scenario: Regression review for a team workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to compare the latest security findings against the existing baseline report. Highlight new risks and resolved issues.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a staged remediation program
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to produce a prioritized remediation plan. Group findings by exploitability, business impact, owner, and recommended verification test.

output_examples.input: Review a Node.js service before release.
output_examples.output: A concise report listing static findings, dependency audit results, secret detection status, OWASP coverage, and prioritized remediation actions.

output_examples.input: Check whether a pull request introduced security regressions.
output: Recommended fixes for each confirmed issue

output_examples.input: Prepare evidence for a security review meeting.
output_examples.output: A markdown audit artifact with scan scope, issue counts, representative findings, and next steps for owners.

best_practices: Run the scanner only on repositories you are authorized to inspect.
Review temporary files and reports before sharing them because they may contain sensitive snippets.
Validate important findings manually before treating them as exploitable vulnerabilities.
anti_patterns: Do not run the skill against broad home directories or unrelated system paths.
Do not publish generated reports without removing secrets and internal paths.
Do not rely on the grep rules as a complete substitute for expert security review.

faq.question: Does this skill perform a full penetration test?
faq.answer: No. It provides a guided multi-phase security review and limited dynamic checks, not a complete penetration test.

faq.question: Can it find every secret in a repository?
faq.answer: No. It uses common patterns and entropy checks, so manual review and dedicated secret scanners are still useful.

faq.question: Why does the audit warn about temporary files?
faq.answer: The helper writes findings to /tmp and generated reports. Those files can contain sensitive code snippets.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish this marketplace skill?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning. The active behavior is consistent with security scanning, but it runs local tools and handles sensitive findings.

faq.question: Which tools does it expect locally?
faq.answer: The shell helper expects common CLI tools including grep, jq, npm, curl, bc, and npx.

faq.question: Can teams use it in CI?
faq.answer: Yes. Teams should restrict scan paths, protect artifacts, and redact sensitive output before uploading reports.
